1:
"And there appeared a great wonder in heaven; a woman clothed with the sun, and the moon under her feet, and upon her head a crown of twelve stars:" The witnesses note the woman clothed with the sun — the crux of her identity. On "there appeared a great wonder in heaven; a woman clothed with the sun," interpreters divide over the woman. The grammatical-historical Barnes, with the Reformed Gill and the latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede, hold the woman is the church — the people of God: Victorinus reads "the ancient Church of fathers, prophets, saints, and apostles," travailing until Christ, the fruit of her people according to the flesh, was born; Bede reads the church clothed with the light of Christ, treading temporal glory; Gill the church in the apostles' and purer ages; Barnes the true church. A second reading (the Roman Catholic/Marian tradition) takes the woman as the Virgin Mary, who bore Christ; the Protestant voices read her as the covenant community, not Mary.

On "clothed with the sun, and the moon under her feet," the witnesses note the glory. Barnes and Ellicott note that the woman is "clothed with the sun" (arrayed in the light and glory of Christ/the gospel) with "the moon under her feet" (the changeable, lower things, or the old dispensation, beneath her); the figure of the church glorious and exalted, treading the mutable world under foot.

On "upon her head a crown of twelve stars," the latin-fathers Victorinus and the grammatical-historical note the crown: "a crown of twelve stars" — the twelve marking the patriarchs or apostles (Victorinus: the choir of fathers from whom Christ was born; others: the twelve apostles); the church crowned with the twelve, the foundation of the people of God. The verse displays the woman clothed with the sun.

The witnesses agree the substance: a glorious woman appears in heaven, sun-clothed, moon-footed, crowned with twelve stars, about to bring forth (Barnes, Gill, Victorinus, Bede). But her identity bears a crux: the church/covenant people of God (the Protestant and patristic consensus — Victorinus, Bede, Gill, Barnes) vs the Virgin Mary (the Catholic/Marian reading). The verse displays the woman crux. The verse marks the wonder: in heaven. The verse displays the woman: sun-clothed. The verse displays the debate: the church or Mary. The verse displays the crown: twelve stars. The vision displays the glorious church bearing Christ. The verse displays the sun: her clothing. The verse displays the moon: under foot. The verse displays the crown: of stars. The woman clothed with the sun displays the opening of the great sign and a crux: a woman arrayed in the sun, the moon beneath her, crowned with twelve stars — read by the Protestant and patristic consensus as the church/covenant people who bring forth Christ (Victorinus, Bede, Gill, Barnes), and by the Catholic tradition as the Virgin Mary. The verse displays the travailing woman, in pain to bring forth (v.2).
2:
"And she being with child cried, travailing in birth, and pained to be delivered." The brief verse notes the woman in travail to bring forth. The grammatical voices note the labor. Barnes and Robertson note the figure of birth-pangs: "she being with child cried, travailing in birth, and pained to be delivered" — the woman is in labor, crying out in the pains of childbirth; the figure of the church's longing, anguish, and effort to bring forth the Messiah (or, more broadly, her children); the travail of the people of God awaiting the promised seed.

On "travailing in birth," the witnesses note the anguish. Barnes and Ellicott note that the travail signifies the suffering and earnest longing of the church/covenant people; in the patristic reading, the groaning of the ancient church for the coming of Christ (Victorinus); the painful, expectant labor before the deliverance.

On "pained to be delivered," the latin-fathers and grammatical-historical note the imminent birth: the woman is "pained to be delivered" — on the point of bringing forth; the long-awaited birth of the man-child (v.5) is at hand; the church's travail is about to issue in the bringing forth of Christ; the anguish precedes the deliverance. The verse displays the woman in travail to bring forth.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the labor — "she being with child cried, travailing in birth, and pained to be delivered," the woman in the pains of childbirth, the church's anguish and longing to bring forth the Messiah (Barnes, Robertson); the travail the suffering and earnest longing of the covenant people, the groaning for Christ's coming (Victorinus, Barnes, Ellicott); "pained to be delivered," on the point of the long-awaited birth (latin-fathers, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the travailing woman. The verse marks the child: with child. The verse displays the travail: in birth. The verse displays the pain: to be delivered. The verse displays the longing: for the Messiah. The labor displays the church anguished to bring forth Christ. The verse displays the woman: travailing. The verse displays the birth: imminent. The verse displays the deliverance: at hand. The woman in travail to bring forth displays the anguish of the expectant church: she cries out in the pains of childbirth, pained to be delivered — the church's longing and travail to bring forth the promised Christ. The verse displays the laboring woman, threatened by the great red dragon (v.3).
3:
"And there appeared another wonder in heaven; and behold a great red dragon, having seven heads and ten horns, and seven crowns upon his heads." The witnesses note the great red dragon with seven heads and ten horns. On "there appeared another wonder in heaven; and behold a great red dragon," the grammatical voices note the adversary. Barnes and Robertson note the second sign: "another wonder (sign) in heaven; and behold a great red dragon" — over against the woman appears a great red dragon; the dragon (identified in v.9 as Satan, the devil) is the great adversary; "red" marks his murderous, bloody character (Victorinus: the color of his murderous work from the beginning).

On "having seven heads and ten horns, and seven crowns upon his heads," the witnesses note the symbols of power. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "seven heads and ten horns, and seven crowns" mark the dragon's great power, dominion, and royalty; the same heads and horns appear on the beast (ch. 13, 17), linking the dragon (Satan) with the worldly persecuting power through which he works; in the historicist reading, the heads and horns are read of Rome (pagan and papal) as the dragon's instrument.

On the dragon, the latin-fathers Victorinus note the character: the dragon's red/purple color reflects his work — "from the beginning a murderer," who has oppressed the whole human race; the great enemy arrayed in power, ready to devour. The verse displays the great red dragon with seven heads and ten horns.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the adversary — "a great red dragon," the great adversary (Satan, v.9), red for his murderous character (Barnes, Robertson, Victorinus); "seven heads and ten horns, and seven crowns," the symbols of great power, dominion, and royalty, linked to the beast (chs. 13, 17), Rome to historicists (Barnes, Ellicott); the dragon "from the beginning a murderer" (Victorinus). The verse displays the dragon. The verse marks the dragon: great, red. The verse displays the heads: seven. The verse displays the horns: ten. The verse displays the crowns: seven. The sign displays the murderous adversary arrayed in power. The verse displays the dragon: appearing. The verse displays the heads: crowned. The verse displays the power: great. The great red dragon with seven heads and ten horns displays the second sign, the adversary of the woman: a great red dragon, marked by murderous color and arrayed with seven heads, ten horns, and seven crowns — the symbols of his great power and dominion; the devil (v.9), working through the worldly persecuting power. The verse displays the dragon, who sweeps the stars and threatens the child (v.4).
4:
"And his tail drew the third part of the stars of heaven, and did cast them to the earth: and the dragon stood before the woman which was ready to be delivered, for to devour her child as soon as it was born." The witnesses note the dragon casting down stars and waiting to devour the child. On "his tail drew the third part of the stars of heaven, and did cast them to the earth," the grammatical voices note the dragon's power and the fall of the stars. Barnes and Robertson note the sweep: "his tail drew the third part of the stars of heaven, and did cast them to the earth" — the dragon's tail sweeps a third of the stars from heaven to earth; interpreters take this of the great power of Satan (drawing down a third), or of his casting down of angels (the fall with him, v.9), or of the casting down of teachers/leaders in the church; the figure of his vast destructive influence.

On "the dragon stood before the woman which was ready to be delivered," the witnesses note the lurking adversary. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the dragon stood before the woman... for to devour her child as soon as it was born" — the dragon waits, poised to destroy the child the moment it is born; Satan's purpose to destroy the Messiah at his birth (Herod's slaughter of the infants, the attempts on Christ's life); the adversary's relentless hostility to Christ.

On "to devour her child as soon as it was born," the puritan Henry notes the design: the dragon's aim was "to devour her child" — to destroy Christ at the outset; Satan, knowing the child would crush him, sought to devour him; the ancient enmity (Genesis 3:15) between the seed of the woman and the serpent comes to its crisis. The verse displays the dragon casting down stars and waiting to devour the child.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sweep — "his tail drew the third part of the stars of heaven, and did cast them to the earth," the dragon's vast destructive power, drawing down a third (the fallen angels, or church leaders, Barnes, Robertson); "the dragon stood before the woman... for to devour her child," Satan poised to destroy the Messiah at birth (Herod, Barnes, Ellicott); "to devour her child as soon as it was born," the ancient enmity of Genesis 3:15 at its crisis (Henry). The verse displays the lurking dragon. The verse marks the tail: drew the stars. The verse displays the cast: to the earth. The verse displays the dragon: before the woman. The verse displays the aim: devour the child. The vision displays Satan seeking to destroy the newborn Christ. The verse displays the stars: cast down. The verse displays the dragon: waiting. The verse displays the child: threatened. The dragon casting down stars and waiting to devour the child displays the adversary's power and malice: his tail sweeps a third of the stars to earth, and he stands before the travailing woman to devour her child the moment it is born — Satan's relentless purpose to destroy the Messiah. The verse displays the threatened child, caught up to God (v.5).
5:
"And she brought forth a man child, who was to rule all nations with a rod of iron: and her child was caught up unto God, and to his throne." The witnesses note the man-child born to rule and caught up to God. On "she brought forth a man child, who was to rule all nations with a rod of iron," the grammatical voices note the birth of the Messiah. Barnes and Robertson note the identification: "she brought forth a man child (son), who was to rule (shepherd) all nations with a rod of iron" — the woman (the church/covenant people) brings forth the man-child, identified as Christ by the Messianic title (Psalm 2:9, "rule with a rod of iron"); the male child destined to rule all nations is the Messiah, born of the people of God.

On "to rule all nations with a rod of iron," the witnesses note the Messianic dominion. Barnes and Ellicott note that the title "rule all nations with a rod of iron" (Psalm 2:9) is distinctively Messianic — Christ's universal, sovereign, irresistible rule over the nations; the child is unmistakably the Christ destined to reign.

On "her child was caught up unto God, and to his throne," the eastern Cyril notes the exaltation: the child "was caught up unto God, and to his throne" — Christ, having been born and having escaped the dragon, is exalted (his ascension to God's throne); the figure compresses Christ's birth, escape from Satan's malice, and ascension/exaltation; the man-child is enthroned with God, beyond the dragon's reach. The verse displays the man-child born to rule and caught up to God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the birth — "she brought forth a man child, who was to rule all nations with a rod of iron," the woman bringing forth the Messiah, marked by the Psalm 2:9 title (Barnes, Robertson); "rule all nations with a rod of iron" the distinctively Messianic dominion, Christ's sovereign rule (Barnes, Ellicott); "her child was caught up unto God, and to his throne," Christ exalted/ascended to God's throne, beyond the dragon's reach (Cyril). The verse displays the man-child. The verse marks the birth: a man child. The verse displays the rule: a rod of iron. The verse displays the identity: the Messiah. The verse displays the exaltation: caught up to God. The birth displays Christ born, escaping the dragon, and enthroned. The verse displays the child: born. The verse displays the rod: of iron. The verse displays the throne: God's. The man-child born to rule and caught up to God displays the birth and triumph of Christ: the woman brings forth the man-child destined to rule all nations with a rod of iron (the Messiah, Psalm 2:9), who is caught up to God and his throne — born, delivered from the dragon, and exalted. The verse displays the enthroned child, while the woman flees to the wilderness (v.6).
6:
"And the woman fled into the wilderness, where she hath a place prepared of God, that they should feed her there a thousand two hundred and threescore days." The witnesses note the woman fleeing to the wilderness for 1260 days. On "the woman fled into the wilderness, where she hath a place prepared of God," the grammatical voices note the flight and refuge. Barnes and Robertson note the church's preservation: "the woman fled into the wilderness, where she hath a place prepared of God" — after the child is born and exalted, the woman (the church) flees to the wilderness, to a place God has prepared; the wilderness signifies a place of refuge, obscurity, and divine protection during persecution; God provides for his church in the time of trial.

On "that they should feed her there a thousand two hundred and threescore days," the witnesses note the period and provision. Barnes and Ellicott note that the church is fed (nourished, sustained) in the wilderness "a thousand two hundred and threescore days (1260 days)" — the same period as the 42 months (11:2) and the witnesses' testimony (11:3); read by historicists as 1260 years of the church's wilderness-state under the apostate power; God sustains his church through the long period of oppression.

On the wilderness-period, the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the parallel: the 1260 days here equal the "time, times, and half a time" of v.14 — the appointed period of the church's flight and preservation; whether literal 3.5 years (preterist/futurist) or 1260 years (historicist), God's care over the church through the whole period is the point. The verse displays the woman fleeing to the wilderness for 1260 days.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the refuge — "the woman fled into the wilderness, where she hath a place prepared of God," the church fleeing to God's prepared place of refuge and protection (Barnes, Robertson); "feed her there a thousand two hundred and threescore days," the church nourished through the appointed period (the same as 11:2-3; 12:14), 1260 years to historicists (Barnes, Ellicott); God's care over the church through the whole period (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the preserved church. The verse marks the flight: to the wilderness. The verse displays the refuge: prepared of God. The verse displays the period: 1260 days. The verse displays the provision: fed there. The flight displays the church sheltered and sustained by God. The verse displays the woman: fleeing. The verse displays the wilderness: refuge. The verse displays the feeding: provided. The woman fleeing to the wilderness for 1260 days displays the preservation of the church amid persecution: after the child's birth, the woman flees to the wilderness, to a place God prepared, where she is fed for 1260 days — God sustaining his church through the long appointed period of trial. The verse displays the sheltered woman, contrasted with the war in heaven (v.7).
7:
"And there was war in heaven: Michael and his angels fought against the dragon; and the dragon fought and his angels," The witnesses note the war in heaven between Michael and the dragon. On "there was war in heaven: Michael and his angels fought against the dragon," the grammatical voices note the conflict. Barnes and Robertson note the battle: "there was war in heaven: Michael and his angels fought against the dragon; and the dragon fought and his angels" — a war in heaven is described, Michael leading his angels against the dragon and his angels; the cosmic conflict between the forces of God and of Satan.

On the identity of Michael, interpreters note the views. Barnes and Ellicott note that "Michael" (the archangel, Daniel 10:13, 21; 12:1; Jude 9) is the leader of God's angelic host; many take Michael as the great archangel, the prince and protector of God's people; some (and the puritan Henry leans) take Michael as a name or title of Christ himself, the captain of the heavenly host, fighting for his church; the leader of the heavenly armies against Satan.

On "the dragon fought and his angels," the puritan Henry notes the opposing host: "the dragon fought and his angels" — Satan and his fallen angels war against Michael and his host; the conflict is between the two angelic armies; the figure of the great spiritual warfare in which the dragon is opposed and overthrown. The verse displays the war in heaven between Michael and the dragon.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the conflict — "there was war in heaven: Michael and his angels fought against the dragon; and the dragon fought and his angels," the cosmic battle of God's angelic host against Satan's (Barnes, Robertson); "Michael" the archangel and leader of God's host (Daniel 10; Jude 9), taken by some as Christ himself (Barnes, Ellicott, Henry); "the dragon fought and his angels," Satan's fallen angels warring against Michael's host (Henry). The verse displays the heavenly war. The verse marks the war: in heaven. The verse displays the leader: Michael. The verse displays the foe: the dragon. The verse displays the armies: the angels. The conflict displays Michael's host warring against Satan's. The verse displays the war: begun. The verse displays the Michael: fighting. The verse displays the dragon: opposed. The war in heaven between Michael and the dragon displays the cosmic conflict behind the church's persecution: Michael and his angels fight against the dragon and his angels — the great spiritual warfare in which Satan is opposed and (v.8) overthrown. The verse displays the heavenly battle, ending in the dragon's defeat (v.8).
8:
"And prevailed not; neither was their place found any more in heaven." The brief verse notes the dragon's defeat and expulsion from heaven. The grammatical voices note the outcome. Barnes and Robertson note the dragon's failure: "and prevailed not; neither was their place found any more in heaven" — the dragon and his angels were defeated; they did not prevail against Michael's host; and their place was no longer found in heaven; they are cast out, losing their standing.

On "prevailed not," the witnesses note the defeat. Barnes and Ellicott note that the dragon "prevailed not" — Satan was overcome in the conflict; despite his power, he could not stand against Michael and the heavenly host; the defeat of the adversary.

On "neither was their place found any more in heaven," the grammatical-historical note the expulsion: "neither was their place found any more in heaven" — the dragon and his angels lose their place in heaven; they are cast out (v.9); Satan, who had access (as the accuser, Job 1; Zechariah 3), is now expelled; the figure of his decisive defeat and loss of standing, leading to his being cast down to earth. The verse displays the dragon's defeat and expulsion from heaven.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the outcome — "and prevailed not; neither was their place found any more in heaven," the dragon defeated and cast out, his place lost (Barnes, Robertson); "prevailed not," Satan overcome, unable to stand against Michael's host (Barnes, Ellicott); "neither was their place found any more in heaven," the dragon and his angels expelled, losing their standing (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the defeated dragon. The verse marks the result: prevailed not. The verse displays the expulsion: no place in heaven. The verse displays the defeat: of Satan. The verse displays the loss: of standing. The outcome displays Satan overcome and cast out. The verse displays the dragon: defeated. The verse displays the place: lost. The verse displays the heaven: cleared. The dragon's defeat and expulsion from heaven display the result of the heavenly war: the dragon and his angels prevailed not, and their place was found no more in heaven — Satan overcome, cast out, and losing his standing. The verse displays the cast-out dragon, hurled down to the earth (v.9).
9:
"And the great dragon was cast out, that old serpent, called the Devil, and Satan, which deceiveth the whole world: he was cast out into the earth, and his angels were cast out with him." The witnesses note the dragon identified as Satan and cast down to earth. On "the great dragon was cast out, that old serpent, called the Devil, and Satan," the grammatical voices note the identification. Barnes and Robertson note the unmistakable naming: "the great dragon was cast out, that old serpent, called the Devil, and Satan" — the dragon is plainly identified: he is the "old serpent" (of Eden, Genesis 3), the "Devil" (diabolos, the slanderer/accuser), and "Satan" (the adversary); the various names mark his character as the ancient enemy, accuser, and adversary of God and man.

On "which deceiveth the whole world," the witnesses note his work. Barnes and Ellicott note that the dragon "deceiveth the whole world (the whole inhabited earth)" — Satan's characteristic work is deception; he leads the whole world astray; the deceiver of mankind, opposed and now cast down.

On "he was cast out into the earth, and his angels were cast out with him," the puritan Henry notes the expulsion: "he was cast out into the earth, and his angels were cast out with him" — Satan and his angels are cast down from heaven to the earth; his defeat in the heavenly war issues in his being hurled to earth, where (vv.12-13) he vents his rage; the figure of his decisive overthrow, yet his continued (limited) activity on earth. The verse displays the dragon identified as Satan and cast down to earth.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the identification — "the great dragon... that old serpent, called the Devil, and Satan," the dragon plainly identified as the ancient serpent, the Devil, and Satan (Genesis 3, Barnes, Robertson); "which deceiveth the whole world," Satan the deceiver of mankind (Barnes, Ellicott); "he was cast out into the earth, and his angels with him," Satan and his angels hurled from heaven to earth (Henry). The verse displays the cast-down Satan. The verse marks the dragon: the Devil, Satan. The verse displays the serpent: old, of Eden. The verse displays the work: deceives the world. The verse displays the expulsion: cast to earth. The identification displays the ancient deceiver overthrown and hurled down. The verse displays the dragon: named. The verse displays the deceiver: exposed. The verse displays the earth: where cast. The dragon identified as Satan and cast down to earth displays the unmasking and defeat of the adversary: the great dragon — the old serpent, the Devil, Satan, who deceives the whole world — is cast out of heaven to the earth, with his angels; the ancient enemy overthrown. The verse displays the fallen Satan, occasioning heaven's triumph-song (vv.10-12).
10:
"And I heard a loud voice saying in heaven, Now is come salvation, and strength, and the kingdom of our God, and the power of his Christ: for the accuser of our brethren is cast down, which accused them before our God day and night." The witnesses note heaven's triumph-song at the casting down of the accuser. On "I heard a loud voice saying in heaven, Now is come salvation, and strength, and the kingdom of our God," the grammatical voices note the proclamation. Barnes and Robertson note the heavenly song: "Now is come salvation, and strength (power), and the kingdom of our God, and the power (authority) of his Christ" — at Satan's overthrow, a loud voice proclaims that salvation, power, and the kingdom of God and the authority of his Christ have come; the victory over the accuser is the triumph of God's kingdom.

On "the accuser of our brethren is cast down, which accused them before our God day and night," the witnesses note the casting down of the accuser. Barnes and Ellicott note the cause of the rejoicing: "the accuser (kategor) of our brethren is cast down, which accused them before our God day and night" — Satan's role as the accuser (Job 1; Zechariah 3:1) of the saints, perpetually charging them before God, is ended; he is cast down, no longer able to accuse; the deliverance of the brethren from their accuser.

On "accused them before our God day and night," the grammatical-historical note the ceaseless accusation: Satan "accused them... day and night" — his relentless, ceaseless accusation of the saints; the casting down of so persistent an accuser is cause for the loud rejoicing of heaven; the brethren are freed from his charges. The verse displays heaven's triumph-song at the casting down of the accuser.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the proclamation — "Now is come salvation, and strength, and the kingdom of our God, and the power of his Christ," heaven proclaiming the triumph of God's kingdom at Satan's overthrow (Barnes, Robertson); "the accuser of our brethren is cast down," Satan the accuser (Job 1; Zechariah 3) cast down, no longer able to charge the saints (Barnes, Ellicott); "accused them before our God day and night," his ceaseless accusation ended (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the triumph-song. The verse marks the song: salvation come. The verse displays the kingdom: of God. The verse displays the accuser: cast down. The verse displays the deliverance: of the brethren. The song displays heaven rejoicing at the accuser's overthrow. The verse displays the salvation: come. The verse displays the accuser: silenced. The verse displays the brethren: freed. Heaven's triumph-song at the casting down of the accuser displays the joy of the victory: a loud voice proclaims that salvation, strength, and the kingdom of God and the power of his Christ have come — for the accuser of the brethren, who charged them before God day and night, is cast down. The verse displays the silenced accuser, overcome by the blood of the Lamb (v.11).
11:
"And they overcame him by the blood of the Lamb, and by the word of their testimony; and they loved not their lives unto the death." The witnesses note the saints overcoming Satan by the blood of the Lamb and their testimony. On "they overcame him by the blood of the Lamb," the grammatical voices note the ground of victory. Barnes and Robertson note the means of conquest: "they overcame him (Satan, the accuser) by the blood of the Lamb" — the brethren overcome Satan, the accuser, by the blood of Christ; the atoning blood of the Lamb is the ground of their victory — it answers every accusation, cleanses from sin, and silences the accuser; the believer's triumph rests on Christ's blood.

On "and by the word of their testimony," the witnesses note their confession. Barnes and Ellicott note that they overcame "by the word of their testimony" — their faithful confession of Christ and the gospel; their steadfast witness, even unto death, is the means by which, in the strength of the Lamb's blood, they overcome the adversary.

On "they loved not their lives unto the death," the eastern Cyril notes the martyr-spirit: "they loved not their lives unto the death" — the overcomers did not cling to their lives but were willing to die for Christ; the martyr's self-sacrifice; their willingness to lay down life for the testimony seals their victory; they conquered by not loving their lives even unto death. The verse displays the saints overcoming Satan by the blood of the Lamb and their testimony.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ground — "they overcame him by the blood of the Lamb," the brethren overcoming the accuser by Christ's atoning blood, which answers every charge (Barnes, Robertson); "by the word of their testimony," their faithful confession and witness the means of victory (Barnes, Ellicott); "they loved not their lives unto the death," the martyr-spirit, willingness to die for Christ sealing their triumph (Cyril). The verse displays the overcoming saints. The verse marks the victory: over Satan. The verse displays the ground: the Lamb's blood. The verse displays the means: their testimony. The verse displays the spirit: loved not their lives. The victory displays the saints conquering by the blood and faithful unto death. The verse displays the overcoming: accomplished. The verse displays the blood: of the Lamb. The verse displays the testimony: held. The saints overcoming Satan by the blood of the Lamb and their testimony display how the accuser is defeated in his people: they overcame him by the blood of the Lamb (which silences every accusation) and by the word of their testimony, not loving their lives even unto death — the believer's victory grounded in Christ's blood and sealed by faithful self-sacrifice. The verse displays the overcoming saints, while the dragon rages on earth (v.12).
12:
"Therefore rejoice, ye heavens, and ye that dwell in them. Woe to the inhabiters of the earth and of the sea! for the devil is come down unto you, having great wrath, because he knoweth that he hath but a short time." The witnesses note the call for heaven to rejoice and the woe to earth at the devil's coming down. On "rejoice, ye heavens, and ye that dwell in them," the grammatical voices note the heavenly joy. Barnes and Robertson note the summons to gladness: "rejoice, ye heavens, and ye that dwell in them" — heaven and its inhabitants are called to rejoice at Satan's casting down; the victory over the accuser is cause for the gladness of the heavenly host.

On "Woe to the inhabiters of the earth and of the sea! for the devil is come down unto you, having great wrath," the witnesses note the woe to earth. Barnes and Ellicott note the contrast: "Woe to the inhabiters of the earth and of the sea! for the devil is come down unto you, having great wrath" — while heaven rejoices, woe falls on the earth, for the devil, cast down, comes with great wrath; his expulsion from heaven turns his fury upon the earth; the earth becomes the scene of his raging.

On "because he knoweth that he hath but a short time," the puritan Henry notes the limited fury: the devil rages "because he knoweth that he hath but a short time" — Satan's wrath is intensified by his knowledge that his time is short; aware of his coming final defeat, he vents his fury the more fiercely in the brief time remaining; his rage is real but limited and doomed. The verse displays the call for heaven to rejoice and the woe to earth at the devil's coming down.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the joy — "rejoice, ye heavens, and ye that dwell in them," heaven called to gladness at Satan's casting down (Barnes, Robertson); "Woe to the inhabiters of the earth... for the devil is come down... having great wrath," woe to earth as the cast-down devil turns his fury there (Barnes, Ellicott); "he knoweth that he hath but a short time," his rage intensified yet limited by his knowledge of his short time (Henry). The verse displays the joy and the woe. The verse marks the rejoicing: of heaven. The verse displays the woe: to earth. The verse displays the devil: come down. The verse displays the wrath: great but short. The contrast displays heaven's joy and earth's woe at Satan's fall. The verse displays the heavens: rejoicing. The verse displays the earth: woe. The verse displays the time: short. The call for heaven to rejoice and the woe to earth at the devil's coming down display the double effect of Satan's overthrow: heaven and its dwellers rejoice, but woe falls on the earth, for the devil has come down with great wrath — raging the more because he knows his time is short. The verse displays the raging devil, who persecutes the woman (v.13).
13:
"And when the dragon saw that he was cast unto the earth, he persecuted the woman which brought forth the man child." The brief verse notes the dragon persecuting the woman after his casting down. The grammatical voices note the turn of his fury. Barnes and Robertson note the renewed assault: "when the dragon saw that he was cast unto the earth, he persecuted the woman which brought forth the man child" — finding himself cast down to earth, the dragon turns his fury against the woman (the church) who bore the man-child (Christ); unable to destroy the child (now exalted, v.5), Satan persecutes the church.

On "he persecuted the woman," the witnesses note the object of his rage. Barnes and Ellicott note that the dragon "persecuted the woman" — the church becomes the target of Satan's wrath; having failed against Christ, he vents his fury on Christ's people; the persecution of the church is the dragon's revenge.

On "which brought forth the man child," the puritan Henry notes the connection: he persecutes "the woman which brought forth the man child" — the church is attacked precisely because she is the mother of the Messiah, the people of God from whom Christ came; the dragon's hatred of Christ is turned against the community that bore and belongs to him. The verse displays the dragon persecuting the woman after his casting down.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the renewed assault — "when the dragon saw that he was cast unto the earth, he persecuted the woman which brought forth the man child," the cast-down dragon turning his fury on the church who bore Christ (Barnes, Robertson); the church the target of Satan's wrath, his revenge for failing against Christ (Barnes, Ellicott); "which brought forth the man child," the church attacked as the mother of the Messiah (Henry). The verse displays the persecuted woman. The verse marks the dragon: cast to earth. The verse displays the persecution: of the woman. The verse displays the object: the church. The verse displays the cause: she bore Christ. The assault displays Satan venting his fury on the church. The verse displays the dragon: raging. The verse displays the woman: persecuted. The verse displays the child: borne. The dragon persecuting the woman after his casting down displays the turn of Satan's fury upon the church: cast down to earth, the dragon persecutes the woman who brought forth the man-child — unable to destroy the exalted Christ, he vents his wrath on the church that bore and belongs to him. The verse displays the persecuted church, given wings to flee (v.14).
14:
"And to the woman were given two wings of a great eagle, that she might fly into the wilderness, into her place, where she is nourished for a time, and times, and half a time, from the face of the serpent." The witnesses note the woman given eagle's wings to flee and be nourished. On "to the woman were given two wings of a great eagle, that she might fly into the wilderness," the grammatical voices note the deliverance. Barnes and Robertson note the divine aid: "to the woman were given two wings of a great eagle, that she might fly into the wilderness, into her place" — the church is given eagle's wings (the figure of swift, God-given deliverance, drawn from Exodus 19:4, "I bare you on eagles' wings") to flee from the dragon to her prepared place in the wilderness; God enables the church to escape the persecutor.

On "where she is nourished for a time, and times, and half a time," the witnesses note the period and provision. Barnes and Ellicott note that the woman "is nourished (sustained) for a time, and times, and half a time" — the same period as the 1260 days (v.6) and the 42 months (11:2); "a time, times, and half a time" (Daniel 7:25; 12:7) = 3.5 "times," read by historicists as 1260 years; the church is sustained by God through the whole period of her wilderness-flight.

On "from the face of the serpent," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the refuge: the woman flees and is nourished "from the face of the serpent" — away from the presence and reach of the dragon; God's care preserves the church from Satan's fury during the appointed time; the serpent cannot reach her in her God-given refuge. The verse displays the woman given eagle's wings to flee and be nourished.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the deliverance — "two wings of a great eagle, that she might fly into the wilderness," the church given swift God-given escape (Exodus 19:4) to her prepared place (Barnes, Robertson); "nourished for a time, and times, and half a time," sustained through the appointed period (= 1260 days, 42 months; Daniel 7:25), 1260 years to historicists (Barnes, Ellicott); "from the face of the serpent," preserved away from the dragon's reach (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the delivered church. The verse marks the wings: of an eagle. The verse displays the flight: to the wilderness. The verse displays the nourishment: the appointed period. The verse displays the refuge: from the serpent. The deliverance displays the church borne to safety and sustained by God. The verse displays the wings: given. The verse displays the wilderness: refuge. The verse displays the serpent: escaped. The woman given eagle's wings to flee and be nourished displays God's preservation of the church: she is given eagle's wings to fly to her wilderness-refuge, where she is nourished for a time, times, and half a time, away from the serpent's reach — God bearing and sustaining his church through the appointed period of persecution. The verse displays the sheltered church, against the serpent's flood (v.15).
15:
"And the serpent cast out of his mouth water as a flood after the woman, that he might cause her to be carried away of the flood." The brief verse notes the serpent's flood loosed against the woman. The grammatical voices note the new assault. Barnes and Robertson note the figure: "the serpent cast out of his mouth water as a flood after the woman, that he might cause her to be carried away of the flood" — the serpent pours out a flood of water from his mouth after the fleeing woman, to sweep her away; the flood is the figure of an overwhelming assault — persecution, false teaching, armies, or the rage of nations — by which Satan seeks to destroy the church.

On "water as a flood," the witnesses note the meaning. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "flood" out of the serpent's mouth is a torrent of opposition; interpreters take it of floods of persecution, or of errors and heresies, or of hostile armies/peoples (waters often symbolizing peoples, 17:15); the overwhelming force loosed to drown the church.

On "that he might cause her to be carried away of the flood," the grammatical-historical note the design: the serpent's aim was to "cause her to be carried away (swept off, destroyed) of the flood" — to overwhelm and destroy the church by the torrent; Satan's renewed attempt to annihilate the fleeing woman; the assault is real and dangerous, but (v.16) is thwarted. The verse displays the serpent's flood loosed against the woman.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the assault — "the serpent cast out of his mouth water as a flood after the woman... that he might cause her to be carried away," the serpent loosing an overwhelming flood to sweep away the fleeing church (Barnes, Robertson); the "flood" a torrent of persecution, heresy, or hostile peoples (waters = peoples, 17:15, Barnes, Ellicott); the aim to overwhelm and destroy the church (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the loosed flood. The verse marks the serpent: casting out water. The verse displays the flood: after the woman. The verse displays the meaning: overwhelming assault. The verse displays the design: to sweep her away. The assault displays Satan's torrent loosed to drown the church. The verse displays the flood: poured out. The verse displays the woman: pursued. The verse displays the destruction: intended. The serpent's flood loosed against the woman displays Satan's renewed assault: the serpent pours a flood from his mouth after the fleeing woman, to sweep her away — the torrent of persecution, error, or hostile peoples by which he seeks to overwhelm and destroy the church. The verse displays the loosed flood, swallowed by the earth (v.16).
16:
"And the earth helped the woman, and the earth opened her mouth, and swallowed up the flood which the dragon cast out of his mouth." The brief verse notes the earth swallowing the flood and helping the woman. The grammatical voices note the deliverance. Barnes and Robertson note the rescue: "the earth helped the woman, and the earth opened her mouth, and swallowed up the flood which the dragon cast out of his mouth" — the earth comes to the woman's aid, opening to swallow the flood; the torrent loosed to destroy the church is absorbed and rendered harmless; God providentially delivers the church from the overwhelming assault.

On "the earth helped the woman," the witnesses note the providential aid. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the earth helped the woman" — some earthly agency or providential circumstance comes to the church's rescue; in the historicist reading, perhaps the absorption of the persecuting flood by the breakup of empires, the swallowing up of the invaders, or the diversion of the hostile powers; God uses even the earth to protect his church.

On "swallowed up the flood," the grammatical-historical note the thwarted assault: the earth "swallowed up the flood" — the torrent is drained away, the assault fails; whatever Satan loosed to drown the church is absorbed and neutralized; the dragon's renewed attempt is thwarted; the church is again preserved. The verse displays the earth swallowing the flood and helping the woman.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the rescue — "the earth helped the woman, and... swallowed up the flood which the dragon cast out of his mouth," the earth absorbing the torrent and delivering the church (Barnes, Robertson); "the earth helped the woman," providential aid, the breakup of persecuting powers to historicists (Barnes, Ellicott); "swallowed up the flood," the assault drained and thwarted, the church preserved (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the swallowed flood. The verse marks the earth: helping. The verse displays the swallowing: of the flood. The verse displays the rescue: of the woman. The verse displays the assault: thwarted. The deliverance displays God preserving the church by swallowing the flood. The verse displays the earth: opening. The verse displays the flood: absorbed. The verse displays the woman: helped. The earth swallowing the flood and helping the woman displays the thwarting of Satan's assault: the earth opens to swallow the flood the dragon loosed, coming to the woman's aid — God providentially absorbing the torrent and again preserving his church. The verse displays the delivered church, against whom the dragon makes war on the remnant (v.17).
17:
"And the dragon was wroth with the woman, and went to make war with the remnant of her seed, which keep the commandments of God, and have the testimony of Jesus Christ." The witnesses note the dragon making war on the remnant of the woman's seed. On "the dragon was wroth with the woman, and went to make war with the remnant of her seed," the grammatical voices note the redirected fury. Barnes and Robertson note the dragon's continued assault: "the dragon was wroth (enraged) with the woman, and went to make war with the remnant (the rest) of her seed (offspring)" — frustrated in destroying the woman herself, the dragon turns to war against the rest of her offspring; unable to destroy the church as a whole, Satan attacks her individual members, the faithful believers.

On "the remnant of her seed," the witnesses note the faithful. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the remnant of her seed" are the woman's other children — the body of faithful believers, the individual Christians who are her offspring; the dragon, foiled against the church corporately, makes war on the saints personally; the ongoing persecution of the faithful.

On "which keep the commandments of God, and have the testimony of Jesus Christ," the grammatical-historical note the marks of the remnant: the seed are those "which keep the commandments of God, and have the testimony of Jesus Christ" — the two marks of the true church and faithful believer: obedience to God's commandments and the holding/witnessing of the testimony of Jesus; these are the objects of the dragon's war and the description of those who, despite his fury, remain faithful. The verse displays the dragon making war on the remnant of the woman's seed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the redirected fury — "the dragon was wroth with the woman, and went to make war with the remnant of her seed," the frustrated dragon attacking the woman's individual offspring (Barnes, Robertson); "the remnant of her seed" the faithful believers, the saints attacked personally (Barnes, Ellicott); "which keep the commandments of God, and have the testimony of Jesus Christ," the two marks of the faithful remnant, obedience and witness (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the warred-on remnant. The verse marks the dragon: wroth. The verse displays the war: on the seed. The verse displays the remnant: the faithful. The verse displays the marks: commandments and testimony. The assault displays Satan warring on the obedient, witnessing saints. The verse displays the dragon: enraged. The verse displays the seed: warred on. The verse displays the faithful: marked. The dragon making war on the remnant of the woman's seed displays the ongoing conflict that closes the chapter: enraged with the woman, the dragon goes to make war on the rest of her offspring — those who keep God's commandments and hold the testimony of Jesus; Satan's war against the faithful saints. The verse displays the persecuted remnant, closing the great sign of the woman and the dragon and setting the stage for the beast (ch.13).
